questions.question: Explain the difference between 0/1 Knapsack and Fractional Knapsack. Why can't we use greedy approach for 0/1 Knapsack?
keyPoints: 0/1: items cannot be divided; Fractional: items can be taken fractionally
Greedy works for Fractional by taking highest value/weight ratio items first
Greedy fails for 0/1 because it may include parts of items leading to suboptimal solutions
Example: items weight=10,value=60 and weight=20,value=100 with capacity 15 - greedy takes first (value=60) but optimal is second (value=100)

questions.question: Define optimal substructure and overlapping subproblems with reference to Knapsack problem.
keyPoints: Optimal substructure: optimal solution contains optimal solutions to subproblems
Knapsack: best value for capacity w using first i items uses best solution for smaller capacity or smaller item set
Overlapping subproblems: same subproblems solved multiple times in recursive approach
DP solves this by storing results in table

questions.question: Write the pseudocode for 0/1 Knapsack using bottom-up DP approach.
keyPoints: Initialize DP table of size (n+1) x (W+1) with zeros
Base case: DP[0][w] = 0 for all w, DP[i][0] = 0 for all i
For i from 1 to n, for w from 1 to W
Apply recurrence: if wt[i-1] ≤ w then DP[i][w] = max(DP[i-1][w], val[i-1] + DP[i-1][w-wt[i-1]]) else DP[i][w] = DP[i-1][w]
Return DP[n][W]

questions.question: Explain the Subset Sum problem. How is it related to 0/1 Knapsack?
keyPoints: Subset Sum: find if any subset of given numbers sums to target S
Similar to 0/1 Knapsack where all values = 1 and target = knapsack capacity
DP state: DP[i][sum] = true if subset of first i elements sums to sum
Recurrence: DP[i][sum] = DP[i-1][sum] OR DP[i-1][sum-arr[i-1]]
Time: O(nS), Space: O(nS)

questions.question: What is space optimization in 0/1 Knapsack? Why do we traverse capacity in reverse order?
keyPoints: Space optimization reduces 2D table O(nW) to 1D array O(W)
Traverse from W down to 0 (reverse order)
Reverse ensures each item is used only once
Going forward would overwrite values needed for current item, causing reuse
1D: for w from W down to wt[i-1]: dp[w] = max(dp[w], val[i-1] + dp[w-wt[i-1]])

questions.question: Compare memoization (top-down) and tabulation (bottom-up) approaches for solving 0/1 Knapsack.
keyPoints: Memoization: recursive approach with caching, starts from main problem, solves only needed subproblems
Tabulation: iterative approach, builds table from base cases, solves all subproblems
Memoization may have recursion overhead, tabulation may compute unnecessary states
Both give same time complexity O(nW), space differs based on implementation

questions.question: Given n=3, W=5, values=[60,100,120], weights=[10,20,30]. What is the maximum value using 0/1 Knapsack? Show the DP table.
keyPoints: Only items with weight ≤5 can fit - none of them individually fit
DP table shows maximum value = 0
No combination fits within capacity 5
Answer: 0 (or empty set is the only valid selection)
This demonstrates importance of checking weight constraints

questions.question: Solve the 0/1 Knapsack problem for the following instance using DP: n=4, W=8, values=[10, 10, 12, 18], weights=[2, 3, 4, 5]. Show complete DP table and trace the solution.
keyPoints: Initialize 5x9 table with zeros
Fill row by row using recurrence relation
DP[4][8] = 18 (optimal value)
Traceback: start from cell (4,8), determine if item 4 included
Complete table shows each decision at each state
Solution: Select items with weights 3 and 5 (values 10+18=28) or weights 2+4+2=8 (10+12=22) - verify optimal

questions.question: Write and explain the algorithm to find all subsets that sum to a given target S using dynamic programming. Also compute all subsets for set {2,3,5,7,10} that sum to 12.
keyPoints: Create DP table where each cell stores list of subsets
DP[i][sum] contains subsets using first i elements that sum to sum
For each element, either include or exclude
Alternatively, backtrack through boolean DP table
Show complete subset enumeration process

questions.question: Prove that 0/1 Knapsack has optimal substructure property. Also explain how you would modify the solution for the bounded knapsack problem where each item has a limited quantity.
keyPoints: Optimal substructure proof: consider optimal solution for n items and capacity W
If item n is not in optimal solution, then solution must be optimal for first n-1 items and capacity W
If item n is included, remaining must be optimal for first n-1 items with capacity W-wn
Proof by contradiction: if sub-solution wasn't optimal, replacing it would improve overall solution
Bounded Knapsack: treat each copy as separate item (inefficient) or use DP with item count dimension
3D DP: DP[i][w][c] = max value using first i items with capacity w and c copies of current item
Or convert to binary representation of quantities for optimization

questions.question: Write a program (in C/Python/Java) to implement 0/1 Knapsack using both recursive memoization and bottom-up tabulation. Compare their performance for different inputs.
keyPoints: Memoization: use dictionary/cache, recursive call with (i,w) parameters
Tabulation: nested loops building 2D table
Code should include: function definitions, base cases, recurrence implementation
For large n, memoization may outperform (solves fewer subproblems)
For small n, tabulation may be faster (no recursion overhead)
Space: memoization uses O(nW) for cache + recursion stack, tabulation always O(nW)
Show sample output comparing both approaches
